1 10/30/06 is the inception date for the oldest class of shares, Class R1 shares. Class R6 shares were first offered on 9/1/11. Returns prior to this date are those of Class R1 shares and have not been adjusted for expenses; otherwise, returns would vary.

2 Comparison against the benchmark and Morningstar category is based on Class R6 shares.

3 It is not possible to invest in an index. Index definitions can be found within the "What you should know before Investing" section on the last page of this fact sheet.

4 “Net (what you pay)” represents the effect of a contractual fee waiver and/or expense reimbursement and is subject to change.

5 Listed holdings reflect the largest portions of the fund’s total and may change at any time. They are not recommendations to buy or sell any security. Data is expressed as a percentage of net assets and excludes cash and cash equivalents.

The performance data shown represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Returns for periods shorter than one year are cumulative, and results for other share classes will vary. Shares will fluctuate in value and, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance cited. Performance figures assume that all distributions are reinvested. For the most recent month-end performance, visit John Hancock Investment Mgmt.

Market review and outlook

Broad-based measures of global equity performance, such as the MSCI World Index, were roughly flat in the fourth quarter. U.S. stocks strongly outperformed their developed-market peers due to the country’s faster economic growth, an election outcome perceived to be market-friendly, and a continued rally in the country’s mega-cap technology sector. On the other hand, the overseas markets posted sizable losses. European and emerging-market stocks lost ground, reflecting slowing global growth and rising protectionism in the United States. Developed Asia, while suffering a loss in absolute terms, outperformed Europe.

Bonds suffered negative total returns in the quarter amid waning optimism about the potential for significant interest-rate cuts by the U.S. Federal Reserve in 2025. Rate-sensitive assets generally produced the weakest returns, but credit oriented market segments were well supported by the backdrop of positive economic growth and hearty investor risk appetites.

Contributors and detractors

The portfolio underperformed in the quarter, but this was within expectations given that market returns remained highly concentrated in a narrow group of U.S. technology stocks. In managing the portfolio, our goal is to provide favorable long-term results. We therefore maintained a steady, diversified approach rather than attempting to chase short-term momentum in certain areas of the market.

Along this line, the portfolio’s underweight in U.S. large-cap stocks detracted from relative performance. An allocation to a real assets portfolio, which is intended to provide a buffer against the impact of inflation over time, was an additional detractor due to weakness in real estate investment trusts, global natural resource equities, and infrastructure stocks. On the positive side, an underweight in the international markets contributed. The bond portfolio experienced a loss and underperformed the fixed-income portion of the benchmark. Allocations to short-term bonds, intermediate-term bonds, and the credit sectors (including multi-sector bonds, bank loans, and emerging-market debt) performed reasonably well, but the contribution was offset by a loss for the portfolio’s position in rate-sensitive U.S. Treasury STRIPS.

Underlying manager performance had a slightly positive impact on performance.

Portfolio changes

We continued to trim the extent of the portfolio’s overweight in equities as the markets hit new highs in November. We maintained a position in defensive equities, which we believe should provide ballast if the economy continues to slow. Although U.S. large-cap stocks still feature strong fundamentals and offer exposure to key innovation themes, we think much of the positive outlook has already been factored into prices. In our view, this makes the case for continued diversification in the portfolio. On the fixed-income side, we trimmed allocations to the credit sectors as yield spreads compressed toward historic lows. We believe an emphasis on higher-quality bonds is appropriate at this stage of the economic cycle.

data.title: Range Resources Intends To Grow Production To 2.6 Bcfe Per Day In 2027 (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Range Resources (NYSE:RRC) reported 2024 results that were in-line with expectations and also provided formal guidance for 2025. Range's 2025 guidance was close to my prior modeling, although it expects slightly lower prices for NGLs and slightly higher expenses than what I had estimated.

Despite this Range may still be able to generate over $850 million in 2025 free cash flow at current strip prices while growing production by 1% compared to 2024.

Some of its 2025 capex budget is going to support growth in future years, so it has outlined a plan to grow production by a further 18% (to 2.6 Bcfe per day) by 2027 with a similar capex budget to 2025 levels.

It then believes that it can maintain production at 2.6 Bcfe per day after 2027 with a lower capex budget, and at $3.75 NYMEX natural gas it may be able to generate $1 billion per year in free cash flow (before cash income taxes).I now think Range is slightly undervalued, with an estimated current value of approximately $40 per share and the potential to increase its value to the high-$40s to low-$50s in a few years.

Updated 2025 Outlook

As previously expected, Range is targeting 2025 production of 2.2 Bcfe per day, which is 1% production growth compared to 2024. It expects its liquids percentage to be over 30%, and I have modeled that at 32% for 2025.

Range is planning to build up additional in-process well inventory during 2025 to support higher levels of production growth in 2026 and 2027.

The current strip for 2025 involves approximately $69 WTI oil and $4.20 Henry Hub natural gas. The Mont Belvieu price for Range's mix of NGLs is approximately $25.60. Based on those prices and Range's differential guidance, it is projected to generate $3.238 billion in 2025 revenues before hedges.

Range's hedges are at a good price (with its natural gas swaps at $4.12), but due to strong natural gas prices those hedges still have a minor amount of negative value.

Commodity

Units

Price Per Unit

$ Million

Natural Gas (Bcf)

545,675,000

40,880,000

$26.25

Oil (Barrels)

2,007,500

$56.50

$113

Hedge Value

-$12

Total

$3,226

Range expects $670 million in capex during 2025, which includes up to $30 million for investment in non-maintenance acreage to boost its inventory and support longer laterals.

Range also mentions that $70 million to $100 million of its 2025 capex budget is for driving production growth after 2025.

Expense

$ Million

Direct Operating Expense

$104

Transportation, Gathering, Processing and Compression

$1,225
Taxes Other Than Income

$28

Cash G&A

$145

Exit Costs

$87

Net Cash Interest

$100

Net Brokered Gas Marketing Expense

$10

Capital Expenditures

$670

Total

$2,369

This results in a projection of $857 million in free cash flow for Range in 2025.

Range is increasing its quarterly dividend to $0.09 per share, so it will now have around $87 million in annual dividend payments.

Range's remaining 2025 free cash flow may go towards share repurchases and repaying its remaining $609 million in May 2025 notes.

Future Growth Plans

Range mentioned plans for significant production growth after 2025. It is aiming for 2.6 Bcfe per day in production for 2027, which would be 18% production growth from 2025 levels.

Range expects to achieve that production growth with around $650 million to $700 million in capex per year and that its D&C maintenance capex (to maintain 2.6 Bcfe per day in production) is estimated at $570 million.

At 2.6 Bcfe per day in production and $570 million in maintenance capex, Range could generate approximately $1 billion in free cash flow at $3.75 Henry Hub natural gas before cash income taxes. This does assume no cost inflation for its expenses, though.

The expected production growth from Range (and other producers) is likely to push natural gas prices down from current levels. Hence, I believe a $3.75 long-term price is a reasonable assumption.

It is also uncertain when Range will start paying significant cash income taxes, although it did mention that it had $1.4 billion in federal NOL carryforwards and approximately $770 million in state NOLs. This may be able to largely shield it from cash income taxes for at least a couple of years. Range currently expects an effective cash tax rate in the high-teens for 2027 and beyond.

Notes On Valuation

Generating $1 billion in pre-tax free cash flow in 2028 would result in a value of $46 per share for Range based on its current share count and a 9% free cash flow (before taxes) yield. This may translate into around a 7.5% free cash flow yield after taxes.

Range may have the ability to repurchase a significant amount of shares going forward though given that its next debt maturity (after May 2025) isn't until 2029.

Share repurchases could theoretically push Range's estimated value into the high-$40s to low-$50s for 2028.

I believe that a roughly $40 per share price is currently reasonable for Range.

Conclusion

Range is projected to generate over $850 million in 2025 free cash flow despite some investment in growth capital for next year. It then believes it can grow production by 18% to 2.6 Bcfe per day by 2027.

Range may then be able to generate around $1 billion in free cash flow per year (before cash taxes) and around $800 million to $850 million per year in free cash flow per year after cash taxes at $3.75 Henry Hub natural gas while maintaining production at 2.6 Bcfe per day.

With share repurchases, this would support a value in the high-$40s to low-$50s, and translates into an estimated current value of around $40 per share.

We maintained a position in defensive equities, which we believe should provide ballast if the economy continues to slow. Although U.S. large-cap stocks still feature strong fundamentals and offer exposure to key innovation themes, we think much of the positive outlook has already been factored into prices. In our view, this makes the case for continued diversification in the portfolio. We believe an emphasis on higher-quality bonds is appropriate at this stage of the economic cycle.

data.title: China Puts The Brakes On Copper Smelters - Will It Ease The Industry's Supply Strain?
data.description:
Original Post

By Sohrab Darabshaw

Throughout 2024, China worked to control its rapidly growing copper smelters, which were lowering profit margins amid a bustling copper market. In response, China has released new, more stringent regulations for the construction of new copper smelters. The move aims to keep excess capacity in check.

However, it’s a slowdown rather than a complete halt to copper smelting. China currently leads the world in refined copper production and is also the world’s largest copper consumer. In fact, analysts expect about half of the global refined metal output for this year to come from China.

Why The Move?

  • Mine Supply Control. Companies building new copper smelters need to have enough mine supply to keep plants running, which they can do through direct ownership or equity stakes. However, only a few Chinese companies are likely to meet this requirement.
  • Import Dependence. Chinese plants bring in about 85% of their concentrate from other countries. In 2023, China made 12 million tons of refined copper, but mined just 1.7 million tons. That same year, China held 41 million tons of copper reserves, 4.1% of the world’s total.
  • Increasing Domestic Resources. To ensure a supply of raw materials, China aims to expand its domestic copper mine resources by 5% to 10% over the next three years.
  • Long-Term Contracts. The government intends to pressure copper smelters into long-term contracts with international miners.

China’s Copper Market Dominance to Continue in 2025

According to a Bloomberg report, eleven ministries signed the order to issue the guidelines. Experts claim the new rules are meant to link all expansions coming in the next few years to ensure sufficient control over the copper ore supply. In the meantime, China has to rely on external supply for its copper needs.

In 2023, China’s mined copper output fell to 1.7 million tons from 1.94 million tons the previous year. Meanwhile, the IndexBox report stated that Beijing’s export value of refined copper showed a similar trend. In 2024, Taiwan was the top export destination, at US $2 billion, followed by South Korea and Vietnam.

Amid Efforts to Increase Production, DRC Becomes Primary Suppliers

Many analysts anticipate that the new rules to cut down on the severe competition China’s copper market faces, with smelters often having to fight it out for raw materials. Because of supply issues, smelters often use low-grade copper, while others spend more time exploring copper mines than producing material.

Some see the new rules as similar to how the Chinese government introduced measures to aid the steel and aluminum sectors, and will likely make it very difficult for new smelters to enter the market.

Meanwhile, the Chinese government aims to reduce buying from other countries by ramping up copper mining at home. Incidentally, China is seeing major returns from its large-scale mining investments in the Democratic Republic of Congo.

According to Reuters, the DRC became China’s leading supplier by a wide margin in 2024, with sales of refined copper increasing 71% year-over-year to 1.48 million metric tons. With Chinese firms leading operations in Congo’s copper belt, the trade between the two nations will likely continue to reshape global copper market dynamics.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

The portfolio underperformed in the quarter, but this was within expectations given that market returns remained highly concentrated in a narrow group of U.S. technology stocks. In managing the portfolio, our goal is to generate favorable long-term results. We therefore maintained a steady, diversified approach rather than attempting to chase short-term momentum in certain areas of the market.

Along this line, the portfolio’s underweight in U.S. large-cap stocks detracted from relative performance. An allocation to a real assets portfolio, which is intended to provide a buffer against the impact of inflation over time, was an additional detractor due to weakness in real estate investment trusts, global natural resource equities, and infrastructure stocks. On the positive side, an underweight in the international markets contributed. The portfolio’s bond allocation experienced a loss but slightly outperformed the fixed-income portion of the benchmark. Positions in short-term bonds, intermediate-term bonds, and the credit sectors (including multi-sector bonds, bank loans, and emerging market debt) contributed.

Underlying manager performance had a slightly positive impact on performance.

Portfolio changes

We continued to trim the extent of the portfolio’s overweight in equities as the markets hit new highs in November. We maintained a position in defensive equities, which we believe should provide ballast if the economy continues to slow. Although U.S. large-cap stocks still feature strong fundamentals and offer exposure to key innovation themes, we think much of the positive outlook has already been factored into prices. In our view, this makes the case for continued diversification in the portfolio. On the fixed-income side, we trimmed allocations to the credit sectors as yield spreads compressed toward historic lows. We believe an emphasis on higher-quality bonds is appropriate at this stage of the economic cycle.

data.title: BDC Weekly Review: Lower Rates Drive Supplemental Dividend Trims
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our BDC Market Weekly Review, where we discuss market activity in the Business Development Company ("BDC") sector from both the bottom-up - highlighting individual news and events - as well as the top-down - providing an overview of the broader market.

We also try to add some historical context as well as relevant themes that look to be driving the market or that investors ought to be mindful of. This update covers the period through the third week of February.

Market Action

BDCs had a good week with a total return of nearly 1% on average in our coverage. Month-to-date, the sector is up around 2% with BBDC in the lead and CGBD lagging. CGBD is going through a merger, which drove a downgrade of the company at one of the banks due to likely additional selling pressure from private investors. BBDC delivered decent earnings numbers and has been undervalued in our view, though the recent run-up does challenge this picture somewhat.

The average BDC valuation has continued to rise, trading near the top of its post-GFC range.

Market Themes

A number of BDCs trimmed their supplemental dividends for 2025. The principal driver of this has been the steady drop in net investment income. The median BDC in our coverage has registered a drop in net investment income of 4.2% so far.

ARCC shows a fairly typical pattern, with a drop in net investment income over the last two quarters.

With the Fed likely on hold till the second half of the year, we should see another drop in net investment income in Q2 in aggregate before we see some stabilization. Overall, short-term rates have fallen around 1%, judging by 3-month Treasury Bills. This should translate into an overall drop in net investment income of around 8-10% on average from the previous rate peak.

In response to a drop in net investment income, a number of BDCs have trimmed their supplemental dividends. GBDC won't pay one at all next quarter while HTGC cut it by a penny.

Base dividends across the sector should be relatively safe, with median coverage of 116% in our coverage. Supplemental dividends, however, will likely continue to be trimmed, over the next quarter at least.

Market Commentary

TSLX reported good results, with a 3.3% total NAV return for the quarter - a bit above the median so far. Adjusted net investment income fell about 5% from the previous quarter. We expect another decline next quarter as the lagged effect of lower SOFR makes its way through the accruals.

Non-accruals fell from the previous quarter but remain above their previous trend.

The company's net realized gain track record is impressive.

Stance and Takeaways

A drop in net investment income has not at all affected the sector from a price perspective, as we suggested earlier it wouldn't with BDC prices up around 7% on average since the Fed cut rates. Investors who exited BDCs when the Fed cut rates may have to buy back at higher prices.

Bargains are few and far between. The old reliable bargain FDUS which we have been overweight, has now moved to a 19% premium, while the average-performer BBDC has rallied to just a 5% discount, about 6% below the median valuation in our coverage. NCDL has also nearly closed its discount, now trading at a 2% discount. At current valuations, we would favor BDC bonds over common shares.

1 12/31/91 is the inception date for the oldest class of shares, Class A shares. Class I shares were first offered on 7/28/03; returns prior to this date are those of Class A shares and have not been adjusted for expenses; otherwise, returns would vary.

2It is not possible to invest in an index. Index definitions can be found within the "What you should know before Investing" section on the last page of this fact sheet.

3“Net (what you pay)” represents the effect of a contractual fee waiver and/or expense reimbursement and is subject to change.

4 Listed holdings reflect the largest portions of the fund’s total and may change at any time. They are not recommendations to buy or sell any security. Data is expressed as a percentage of net assets and excludes cash and cash equivalents.

Theperformancedatashownrepresentspastperformance,whichdoesnotguaranteefutureresults.Returnsfor periodsshorterthanoneyeararecumulative,andresultsforothershareclasseswillvary.Shareswillfluctuatein value and, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higherthantheperformancecited.Performancefiguresassumethatalldistributionsarereinvested.Forthemost recentmonth-endperformance,visitJohn Hancock Investment Mgmt.

Market review and outlook

Most segments of the U.S. bond market sold off in the quarter, dampening total returns for the calendar year. Economic growth expectations improved, due in part to the outcome of the November U.S. elections. The U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) made two quarter-point interest-rate cuts, adding to the half-point reduction it enacted in September. Bonds nevertheless came under pressure from concerns about stubborn inflation, as well as Fed Chair Jerome Powell’s hawkish comments on the likely path of monetary policy in 2025.

U.S. Treasuries were adversely affected by these developments, with longer-term bonds experiencing the weakest results. The yield on the 10-year note surged from 3.81% at the end of September to 4.58% on December 31. Mortgage-backed securities ('MBS'), which tend to have elevated sensitivity to the direction of the Treasury market, sold off in response. Asset-backed securities ('ABS') and commercial mortgage-backed securities outpaced MBS at a time in which investors displayed a preference for higher-yielding, credit-oriented categories. Investment-grade corporates also suffered losses even though yield spreads continued to decline toward multi-year lows.

Contributors and detractors

The fund’s yield curve positioning, primarily its overweight in the 5-to 10-year portion of the curve, hurt its fourth-quarter relative performance. The resulting shortfall was offset, to some extent, by a contribution from asset allocation. The fund benefited from its underweight in U.S. Treasuries and overweights in investment-grade corporates and ABS. An overweight in agency MBS detracted. Security selection contributed to results, with the majority of the positive impact occurring in the investment-grade corporate space.

Portfolio changes

We maintained a stable portfolio positioning in the final three months of the year, with minor shifts that were driven largely by bottom-up selection. We added slightly to corporates and ABS, while making a modest reduction in U.S. Treasuries. We kept a significant overweight in agency MBS on the view that the category continued to offer compelling relative value and a source of higher quality. Duration (interest-rate sensitivity) remained neutral versus the benchmark, and the fund was overweight in intermediate-term bonds in anticipation of additional steepening in the yield curve.

In corporates, we maintained an overweight in financials on the belief that the sector was attractive on a relative basis. We also continued to focus on the non-cyclical and utilities sectors, while moving up in quality and focusing on shorter and intermediate maturities. The corporate yield curve was relatively flat, meaning that there was little benefit to taking on added risk by moving further out on the maturity spectrum. In addition, we believed an emphasis on higher-quality, shorter-dated debt could provide a measure of ballast if market conditions became less favorable. With corporate yield spreads at the tight end of the historic range, we maintained the view that income and security selection would be paramount. Although we didn’t look to increase the portfolio’s overall risk profile, we sought opportunities to add incremental yield through individual securities offering attractive relative value.

data.title: Rocket Lab: It's Actually Rocket Science
data.description:
Space stocks have been a wild ride over the past six months and that is no different than this upcoming rocket launcher - Rocket Lab (NASDAQ:RKLB). This end to end space company provided 16 launches in 2024 - an increase of 60% from 2023. And from recent commentary on their most recent launch, and subsequent conferences, they have a manifest of over 20 launches scheduled for 2025. That includes Electron, Haste and not to mention, the newcomer on the scene, the Falcon 9 competitor, Neutron. Retail is keeping a close eye on the first Neutron launch, which is tentatively scheduled for no earlier than mid-year, and has held this timing for the past 3 quarters. Each $55m launch for Neutron is the equivalent of 6.5 Electron launches and will be a catalyst for getting Rocket Lab to $1bn in annual run rate by 2027.

Rockets are not the only service this company provides, as space systems revenue provided over 70% of the company’s topline over the trailing twelve months and the systems division is expected to grow nearly 80% year-on-year. Collectively, Launch and Space Systems have crossed the $100m per quarter threshold three times now, with the 4Q actuals print of $132m. I predicted revenue of $125m.

The recent Bleecker Street short report, while thorough, makes assumptions on cash burn that are extreme, coupled with unwarranted claims of Neutron being delayed by over 2 years. They continue to bash demand for Neutron exclaiming that the medium lift rocket will have little to no demand as Falcon 9 and Starship will own the entirety of the market, meanwhile dedicated Falcon 9 launches go 80% underutilized based on 3 years of data thus a cheaper option (like Neutron) would save companies 20-25% per launch.

All in all, the stock has retraced almost 35% since its peak in January 2025 as the company takes a breather from its 8x run over the last 10 months. Street analysts have been unwavering in their rating to buy the stock and have a $25.50 price target. I have a standing buy on the company and dollar cost average with every influx of capital I get. I anticipate by year-end we see north of $40 on the stock price, or close to a $20bn market cap.

Company Overview

Established in 2006, Rocket Lab has grown over the years into an end to end space company. End to end meaning Rocket lab has the capability to create spacecraft and spacecraft solutions by providing whole satellites, satellite subsystems, launch capability, onboard software and flight management. Rocket Lab’s wholly owned subsidiary SolAero is one of the premier satellite solar companies in the world. The company’s small launch vehicle, Electron, has launched over 60 times since 2017 and the company will debut their medium launch vehicle, Neutron, in the next 12 months. The company went public via SPAC in August 2021 via Vector acquisition corp.

Follow-up from my previous Rocket Lab article

In August 2024, I wrote my 2nd article on Rocket Lab “With Due Haste” where I exclaimed that Rocket Lab had a strong cash position of $540m and any positive news on Neutron would be a catalyst to shoot the stock to $15/share or $7.5bn.

Since that time, Neutron has received 2 separate contracts, and the rocket has not even had a chance to launch yet. This demonstrates the market demand for alternatives other than Falcon 9 in spite of the recent Bleecker Short Report.

Since the August time, I have also recognized that the barriers to space and initial capital needed to be successful are a minimum of $10bn for an end to end space company trying to achieve what Rocket Lab is achieving. Thus the minimum share price for Rocket Lab, with the space systems and launch capability, should be $15 per share - $7.5bn valuation. With a successful Neutron rocket, the value should be closing on $15bn.

The Bleecker Street Short Report

The claims of this short report are unwarranted and easily debunked. The report goes into extensive detail and claims unnamed previous executives and engineers from Rocket Lab, all miraculously ahead of the FY2024 earnings report this week. Bleecker highlights key risks to delivery for Rocket Lab - I have similar risks on my list, but what I do not anticipate is stacking these risks to an almost cataclysmic event like what Bleecker Street represents in their report. The report has claims of all out fraud and misleading investors. On a quarterly basis, the Rocket Lab earnings presentations provide context and progress on outgoing satellites or Neutron progress. These slides and explanations are a few steps shy of a university course, and the Q&A section of the call are generally the longest I experience every quarter - hard to imagine how you can mislead investors with so much detail.

I do not have the time to go line item by line item, but let me start off with facts - Rocket Lab’s ability to execute is second to none, with maybe the exception of SpaceX. The capital efficiency of this organization is astonishing and if other companies had the abilities and frugality of Rocket Lab, there would be dozens of Electron competitors. There are maybe a handful, and most have launched less than 5 times and/or launched once. To date, Electron has launched 60 times. In 2024, Electron reached 50 launches faster than any other vehicle in history, including the Falcon 9. Electron is also on pace to hit 100 launches the fastest as well, beating Falcon 9’s pace by 1 year. This does not guarantee Neutron will have a technically successful launch, but it does demonstrate that the company has had inaugural launches on tight timelines with significantly smaller budgets than competition and have made it work.

Neutron

Delays in the launch business are almost inevitable, and the 4th quarter call there was a slight delay from mid-year 2025. Bleecker’s report claiming that Rocket Lab “guaranteed” a mid-2025 launch, however, is unwarranted. Then stating years of delay and lack of commercial demand. Once again, unwarranted.

I have heard a multitude of times on Rocket Lab calls and Q&A’s that there was a mid-year 2025 target, but this is the rocket industry and with that, potential for delays. I have heard this recently as Adam Spice, company CFO, spoke at the Citi Industrial conference. He noted NET mid-2025. NET meaning No Earlier Than, common language in the industry. I have heard this on countless earnings calls with Sir Peter Beck and Adam, and I’ve heard this on personal recorded calls with Peter and Adam, videos that I have uploaded live on YouTube. We heard notes again yesterday, they stated they needed a couple of months more than mid-year, not years, to get Neutron off the ground. It’s rocket science, there can be delays.

While I agree the timeline to mid-2025 was aggressive and a small mishap can easily push Neutron to late 2025 or early 2026, progress reports and photos are frequent, steady and transparent from the company. Multiple shares of different engine tests, pad progress, and tank testing, are shared as they come. That’s not to say there are a few key items outstanding to be shared, like pad completion, key static fire tests and wet integration of the entire rocket. I agree with Peter, these seem like items that push launch months away, not years.

Discussions of the short report around lack of water, facilities, transport to pad, etc, once again, unwarranted. Launch Complex 3 in Wallops, Virginia, is in NASA’s owned and operated launch range. NASA has existing facilities nearby that has never experienced issues like this in the past, and the pad has serviced commercial and government customers for quite some time. This was called out in the 4th quarter earnings call by CEO, Peter Beck when he exclaimed “yes, and we even have water” when discussing facility progress at Launch Complex 3.

Also, pages 36 through 47 of the Neutron Payload user’s guide explain thoroughly how Rocket Lab customer payloads and Rocket stages will be transported to Rocket Lab’s Launch Complex 3 in Wallops, Virginia. A snippet has been provided below, but further details can be found in the guide.

Demand

The lack of commercial demand stated by Bleecker is centered around two items. Firstly, conversations had by nameless former executives and industry experts where they speculate who is the confidential commercial customer to first order a Neutron launch. And secondly, the Neutron lift capacity being inferior to the Falcon 9, with Neutron being 6,000-15,000kg depending on location in orbit and how the first stage is used versus the 17,000-22,000 kg max touted by Falcon 9.

To provide context here - Neutron, prior to an inaugural launch, has two current customers in the backlog. One, the confidential customer, speculated to be E-space, has ordered 2 launches. One in 2026 and another in 2027. We saw this hit the backlog during the 4Q earnings. The other customer is NASA, with an announcement in January - unfortunately we will have to wait until May earnings call for this to hit the backlog, but we saw the noted customer demand in the 4th quarter earnings call. This VADR, or Venture Class Acquisition of Dedicated and Rideshare launch services contract, was not even mentioned in the Bleecker report. With the report solely focusing on the confidential customer and the ability for it to be ready and pay for a 2026/2027 launch.

The Constellation Is Getting Closer

Additional context that was not noted in the Bleecker report, half of Neutron’s lifts are expected to be for Rocket Lab’s own constellations. This is comparable to what Falcon9 provides to SpaceX’s starlink, and I have had this chat before in personal interviews with Peter and Adam about this in post-earnings discussions.

To give Bleecker some credit, the constellation, while notional prior to the 4th quarter earnings call, had not been fully outlined by the company. Rocket Lab has demonstrated its capability to develop constellations in the past, though, and seeing this as a future revenue stream for the company, in my expectation, is not far-fetched.

The company took the opportunity yesterday to provide the first official mention of the Flatellite. After building constellations for a multitude of business and government customers, Rocket Lab used the requests and criteria used by those customers to create an ideal constellation satellite for national security, defense and commercial customers. This alone should have provided a larger bump in the stock, however with the ongoing macro-economic environment in February 2025, the rest of the high growth market has also been pulled down. More will come on this and I am sure it will bear more fruit.

Neutron Payload discrepancy vs. SpaceX's Falcon 9

Back to payload discrepancy - the short report states why choose a Neutron rocket for $55m, when it has less lift capacity than Falcon 9 for approximately $70m? This is a very general view of the capacities and prices - while the capacity of Falcon 9 is greater, the majority of F9 customers fill 20-30% of the Falcon 9’s capacity, well within the capacity for Neutron to get to orbit.

Therefore, the average price to get a kg of payload into space for Neutron is $16,000/kg compared to an almost $21,000/kg for Falcon 9 - a 20% savings per kg to space and can accommodate the average SpaceX customer. Not much of a problem when you view it in this perspective, is there?

Haste - So It Begins

The long awaited 2nd launch for Haste as a hypersonic test bed (MACH-TB) came. In November, Haste launched from launch complex 2, creating an eventual contract with Kratos. While the future Haste launch manifest has not been outlined publicly by the company, this is the first of several customers that Haste will launch for in 2025 and 2026, and it was the first Haste launch in 18 months. I anticipate as the other 3 customers get prepared and aligned, we will see more development in this space. I anticipate this is an underappreciated segment of the company and could have hypersonic rockets launched globally or throughout the US rather than only in LC-2 in Wallops, Virginia.

Let’s Get To The Numbers

Rocket Lab market capitalization is approximately $10-11bn at the time of writing, with a forecasted FY2024 sales of $436m. That leads to sales of approximately 23-25x FY 24 earnings - rich. Knowing that with growth companies we look at metrics 12-24 months out, we see price to sales dip to 17.5x earnings in FY 2025 and 11.5x in FY 2026. Not as outlandish as the FY24 valuation. With the company’s top quartile performance and potential catalysts I can understand the premium put on the stock. That said, on a typical financial valuation, the stock is expensive and cash flow break-even is not anticipated to start until 1H 2026. Meaning, there are currently no earnings or positive EBITDA to do a proper P/E or P/EBITDA measurement.

That said, we know SpaceX also trades at a rich P/S valuation due to its growing revenue base, and the competition in Space is fierce, so anybody that can execute and execute well deserves some premium multiple. That said, in the event of failures or delays, this premium can have the opposite effect on the stock price and provide an elevator for the price to go down. This could be a deterrent to investing in the stock - understandably.

Looking forward even more, as Neutron picks up steam in 2027 and beyond, the valuation for Rocket Lab may seem cheaper here. So as we progress into the latter half of 2025 with a successful Neutron launch, it may be prudent to revisit, knowing that a successful Neutron launch will likely lead to a handful of launches in 2027 and a few short of a dozen in 2028. A decent growth rate if coupled with potential Haste and Space systems business, leading to a cheaper multiple down the line. Three street analysts currently have revenues of $1.25b in 2027, dropping P/S back to 8x, to me, meaning room for growth in the event of a successful Neutron launch.

How does Wall Street and Seeking Alpha view the stock?

I, like Wall Street and Seeking Alpha contributors, have a buy rating on the stock. This is down from a strong buy as the stock has increased 200% since my August 2024 article, and we still have not launched Neutron. While I have a year end price target on the stock, my PT stands 15% above the high range of Street Analysts of $35 and 55% above the average price target for Street analysts.

Peer Analysts on Seeking Alpha and I are in agreement, as the stock surpassed $15 the average rating changed from a strong buy to buy. With a rating of 3.8 out of 5 on Rocket Lab, SA analysts have 2 strong buy recommendations, 5 buy recommendations, 2 holds and 1 sell. I am happy to see more SA analysts covering the stock, 10, up from 3.

Wall Street analysts remain a buy with a 4.2 out of 5 rating. 7 strong buys, 1 buy, and 5 holds in the last 90 days. There are no sellers at this time.

The Street has updated the 52-wk price target from $7.39 to $25.50, representing a nearly 250% increase in the target price and upwards of 25% increase from today’s price of $21. The street also updated the downside from under $5 to its current price of $13.50. The upside has increased from $10 to $35, a 250% increase.

Risk Thesis

Over the last few articles, I have maintained my key risks. Electron customers continue to push launch windows, Neutron was pushed 6 months at a minimum over the past 12 months, but gross margins continue to grow up to the 30% mark.

Fiscal year 2024 had 16 launches for the year, less than the 22 launches outlined in the 2024 manifest and less than the 18 launch ambition outlined in the 3Q call. This loss of launches resulted in a decrease in revenue from the mid-$400m range to end up at $436m. At the average sales price of $8.5m per launch, 6 launches mean a loss of $50m in revenue. That noted, there seemed to be space systems growth that did offset the revenue shortfalls in FY24 to end up in the mid-$430m range.

Non-GAAP gross margins have steadily stayed at 30% over the past few quarters, and climbed to 34% in the 4th quarter, pushing FY24 non-GAAP margins to 32%, up from 28% in FY2023. So I see no risk short term here, but will keep an eye on future quarters. Analysts on the Q&A section are key to ask about legacy contracts with lower gross margins and Adam has been upfront that those contracts are rolling off within the next few quarters so margins should expand after that since he has not seen a sub 30% margin contract in some time.

Initially, the stock dipped from a further Neutron delay beyond mid-2025. However, the stock recovered overnight as the market digested that the delay seems to be a few months, not years. The company continues to make steady progress, posting hot fire and tank testing, coupled with drone footage of Launch Pad 3 at Wallops, Virginia, but key tests like stage 1 static fire, integrated dry testing, wet testing and regulatory approvals are all yet to be completed.

Conclusion

The stock has retraced 35% after an expansive 800% run over the last 10 months or so. This price appreciation coincides with a strong revenue increase of 120% from 4Q 2023 to 4Q 2024 and 78% fiscal year 2023 to fiscal year 2024. The stock continues to demonstrate a resilient run in a high cost, competitive industry like Space. Rocket Lab continues to remain the #2 most launched vehicles in the western hemisphere, touting a 60% increase in launches. Exerting its dominance as the company grows into the go-to contractor for launch services, space systems and soon to be space services. While no 2025 manifest has been provided yet, the Neutron rocket is slated for launch a few months later than mid-year 2025. I anticipate the launch could be pushed further as the remaining list of items to complete is pretty long, but Rocket Lab’s transparency will continue to shine through if they believe the target is unachievable.

Bleecker Street short report has unwarranted claims of management misleading investors and of poor demand for medium launch, meanwhile the report does not acknowledge key recent NASA awards granted to Rocket Lab. Yet, the report was timely enough to be placed ahead of the 4Q earnings call. Under the surface, Neutron is a more cost-effective competitor to the Falcon 9 than the Space community may believe.

Haste and Space Systems are the quiet parts of the business that continue to grow without questions. Space systems accounted for over 70% of the trailing twelve months revenue and continues to expand its footprint in the industry with deliveries of Escapade, and other ongoing crucial space missions (Blue Ghost, etc).

Financially the stock is overvalued, with a high price to sales ratio, however the potential revenue stream when Neutron ramps up cadence is expansive and is a key milestone for the future of the company. Investors that were previously hesitant to invest prior to Neutron success may be wise to look at the forward potential post-Neutron success, as the story will change rapidly for the better.

I remain extremely bullish on the company for a 10-year hold, but will state a $40 price target by year-end 2025 assuming a successful Neutron launch and path towards profitability anticipated in the 1H of 2026. This aligns well with current Street and Seeking Analysts thoughts on a buy, however they have an average price of $25.50.
